description: The provided image is unreadable and appears to be a generic placeholder icon; therefore, this description relies on the accompanying text extraction summary. The figure likely concerns scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs or microstructural characterization data for low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (alloys S1–S5, with 0.60–1.10 wt.% C, 3.92–8.08 wt.% Al, plus Mn, Si, Mo, B, Ni, Ti) subjected to austenitizing at 1150–1250 °C and austempering at 270–365 °C. It probably illustrates nanoscale bainitic ferrite lamellae (34–68 nm thick), retained austenite (19–34 vol.%), and micro-strain distributions (εγ up to 0.58 %, εα up to 0.60 %). Notable differences include 8.2 % polygonal ferrite in high-Al S3, TiMoC and AlN precipitates in Ti-microalloyed S4, and sporadic lenticular martensite in S5 at 335 °C. The overall trend shows that higher austempering temperatures coarsen lamellae and increase austenite stability, while high Al content retards kinetics. These microstructural findings correlate with tensile strengths of 1315–1590 MPa and total elongations of 2.4–10 %, supporting the design of high-strength low-density bainitic steels.

This figure presents the heat treatment protocol and dilatometric methodology for low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels. Panel (a) is a schematic of the austenitizing–rapid cooling–austempering thermal cycles applied to samples S1–S5: heating at 10 K s⁻¹ to austenitizing temperature T_A (1150 °C for S1, S2 and S5; 1220 °C for S3; 1150 or 1250 °C for S4), holding for 15–20 min, then cooling at 100 K s⁻¹ to the bainitic isothermal holding temperature T_B selected just above the martensite-start temperature Ms (ranging from 270 °C to 365 °C depending on the alloy). Panel (b) shows a representative dilatometric analysis where the black curve records relative length change (RLC, %) and the red curve records the instantaneous transformation rate (s⁻¹); the dashed vertical line marks the selected bainitic transformation finish time (Bt) defined as the onset of the RLC plateau where the transformation rate decreases to a negligible level. Together, the panels establish the experimental framework for suppressing intermediate phase transformations and quantifying near-full bainitic transformation kinetics via dilatometry.

Figure 2 presents the combined experimental and computational method used to quantify the retained austenite volume fraction Vγ in nano-bainitic S1 steel (0.98 C, 6.00 Al, wt.%) after austempering at 350 °C. Panel (a) shows the X-ray diffraction pattern indexed with α-phase reflections—(110)α, (200)α, (211)α, and (220)α—alongside γ-phase peaks labeled (111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, and (311)γ; the inset highlights the integrated intensity Iγ of the (200)γ reflection in the yellow-shaded region, which serves as the measured austenite signal for the direct-comparison equation. Panel (b) plots MatCalc-simulated equilibrium phase fractions versus temperature for the same alloy, displaying the relative stability of bcc ferrite, κ-carbide, hcp carbide, (Fe,M)xCy, and AlN on a logarithmic phase-fraction axis. A grey band marks the bainitic transformation window around TB′ = 300–350 °C, and the arrow indicates (1–Vc) = 0.78, representing the matrix fraction remaining after accounting for carbides. Collectively, these panels establish that accurate Vγ determination in this high-Al steel requires coupling XRD integrated intensities with thermodynamic correction for competitive carbide precipitation.

This figure combines dilatometric heating curves (left column) with light optical microscopy (LOM) images (middle and right columns) for low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels S1–S5. The left column plots relative length change (RLC %) versus temperature, with first derivatives d(RLC)/dT in red used to define an operational austenitizing temperature T_A where the derivative peak decays and linearity is restored: 1062 °C (S1), 1031 °C (S2), 1067 °C (S4), ~900 °C (S5), while S3 exhibits a broadened transition without a unique T_A. The middle column shows LOM microstructures at a benchmark 1050 °C, evidencing polygonal ferrite (α_p) retention in S1, S3, S4, and S5, and an α_p-free structure in S2. The right column displays LOM at selected T_A values—1150 °C for S1, S2, S4, and S5, and 1220 °C for S3—demonstrating complete α_p elimination in S1 and S2, reduction to trace levels (≲1 %) in S4 and S5, and persistent α_p (~8.2 % area fraction) in S3 even at elevated temperature. Collectively, these data establish 1150 °C as an optimized austenitizing temperature for S1, S2, S4, and S5 to suppress α_p prior to bainitic transformation, whereas the high-Al S3 steel requires 1220 °C yet retains residual α_p.

Figure 4 presents MatCalc equilibrium phase-fraction–temperature diagrams for five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (S1–S5). Each subplot plots logarithmic phase fraction versus temperature, displaying the equilibrium amounts of bcc ferrite (green), fcc austenite (red), κ-carbide (triangles), hcp carbide (squares), and (Fe,M)xCy MC-type carbides (circles); S4 additionally contains a TiC curve (blue). Grey boxes highlight the ferrite-free austenitizing temperature TA (991 °C for S1, 929 °C for S2, 1177 °C for S3, 1016 °C for S4, and 986 °C for S5) and the carbide-dissolution temperature Tcd (1103–1107 °C for S1–S2, 1103 °C for S3, 1134 °C for S4, and 775 °C for S5). The diagrams reveal that S3 requires a markedly higher TA because of its elevated Al content, whereas S5 exhibits the lowest Tcd, indicating earlier MC carbide dissolution. These calculated dissolution sequences guided the selection of practical austenitizing temperatures to achieve a ferrite-free, chemically homogeneous austenite prior to bainitic transformation.

The figure presents five cooling dilatometry curves plotting Relative Length Change (RLC, %) versus Temperature (°C) for low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels, used to determine the martensite-start temperature (Ms) via the tangent–intersection method on linear contraction segments. Subfigure S1 shows Ms = 277 °C; S2 shows Ms = 274 °C; S3 shows the lowest Ms = 258 °C; and S5 shows the highest Ms = 331 °C. Subfigure S4 overlays two curves for the Ti-microalloyed grade austenitized at 1150 °C (black, Ms = 305 °C) and 1250 °C (red, Ms = 310 °C), indicating only a marginal effect of austenitizing temperature. The systematic Ms variation across S1–S5 reflects differences in effective carbon enrichment of austenite governed by alloy chemistry and dissolution behavior during austenitization. These measured Ms values support the heat-treatment strategy of selecting austempering temperatures slightly above Ms to promote bainitic transformation while suppressing martensite formation.

Figure 6 presents dilatometric bainitic transformation kinetics for low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels, plotting relative length change (RLC, %) versus time. Panels (a) and (b) use logarithmic time axes: (a) compares S1–S3 (Al ≥ 6 wt.%) austempered at 270–350 °C after austenitizing at 1150–1220 °C, while (b) compares S4–S5 (Al < 6 wt.%) at 300–365 °C. Subfigures (c)–(e) replot these data on linear axes with vertical dashed lines marking approximate bainite finish times (Bt). Panel (c) shows S1 and S2 with Bt ranging from 1600 s to 4000 s; (d) isolates sluggish S3 kinetics with Bt reaching 43000 s at 270 °C; and (e) displays faster S4 and S5 transformations, where S5 completes in ~700–1000 s. The data reveal that higher Al strongly retards transformation, TiMoC-containing S4 exhibits accelerated nucleation but subsequent impingement suppression, and S5 achieves the fastest kinetics.

Figure 7 presents a comprehensive mechanical property comparison of five low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (S1-S5) subjected to various austenitization (T_A) and austempering (T_B) conditions. The multi-axis plot displays ultimate tensile strength UTS (black squares, left axis), yield strength Rp0.2 (blue downward triangles, right axis), elongation El (red triangles, right axis in percent), and hardness (black circles, left axis with HV30 values labeled below each condition ranging from 443 to 500). Evaluated conditions span T_A = 1150-1220 °C and T_B = 270-365 °C. S1 and S2 attain peak UTS values near 1600 MPa at lower T_B (300 °C) but with reduced ductility. S3 requires an elevated T_A of 1220 °C due to high Al content and shows the lowest elongation at 270 °C. Notably, S4 exhibits the best strength-ductility balance, achieving approximately 10% elongation with a maximum reaching 12% per caption at T_A = 1150 °C and T_B = 350 °C while maintaining UTS near 1360 MPa. Hardness remains comparatively stable across alloys and treatments (~450-500 HV30). The figure demonstrates that alloy design and austempering temperature critically govern the strength-elongation trade-off in nano-bainitic steels, with Ti-microalloyed S4 offering superior mechanical property synergy.

Figure 8 presents MatCalc-simulated Time-Temperature-Precipitation (TTP) diagrams for the investigated nano-bainitic Al–C steels, comprising four panels: (a) S1 (approximately representative also for S2), (b) S3, (c) S4, and (d) S5. Each panel plots temperature versus logarithmic time with 5 %, 50 %, and 95 % precipitation curves for (Fe,M)xCy carbides under a cooling rate of 100 Ks−1 from specified austenitizing temperatures (T_A). Subfigure (a) for S1 uses T_A = 1150 °C and reports bainite transformation finish times (Bt) of 2300 s at T_B = 350 °C and 3600 s at T_B = 300 °C. Subfigure (b) for S3 uses T_A = 1220 °C, showing prolonged Bt of 14 000 s at 350 °C and 43 000 s at 270 °C. Subfigure (c) for S4 features a two-step austenitizing profile (1250 °C, then 1150 °C for 900 s) and distinctly overlays TiMoC and (Fe,M)xCy precipitation zones (shaded grey), with Bt values of 2600–3000 s at 350 °C and 3500 s at 320 °C. Subfigure (d) for S5 (T_A = 1150 °C) exhibits the fastest kinetics, with Bt = 1000 s at 365 °C and 700 s at 335 °C. The diagrams confirm that (Fe,M)xCy precipitation during cooling is unavoidable in S1 and S3 between approximately 700 °C and 1150 °C, whereas in S4 the Ti–Mo–C precipitate system suppresses (Fe,M)xCy growth, shifting its TTP nose to longer times and enabling controlled microstructural design.

The figure comprises SEM and LOM micrographs characterizing low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (grades S1–S5). SEM subfigures display nanoscale bainitic ferrite lamellae with inter-lath retained austenite and (Fe,M)xCy cementite-like carbides located at phase boundaries, annotated by white arrows and specimen labels (Spec.1 through Spec.6). Subtle morphological variations across the panel suggest differences in lamellar spacing and packet coarseness among alloys. Some subfigures additionally highlight coarser precipitate assemblies and grain-boundary films labeled as 3, 4, 5, and 6, indicating heterogeneous phase distribution. The LOM insets with 50 μm scale bars reveal broader microstructural contexts, including phase volume fractions and grain-scale arrangement. Together, the images illustrate how austempering in the 270–365 °C range produces nano-bainitic structures with alloy-dependent secondary phases and ferrite morphologies, underscoring the microstructural basis for the mechanical property variations reported in the study.

This figure comprises scanning electron micrographs (a–c) and energy-dispersive X-ray elemental maps (d) for Ti-microalloyed nano-bainitic steel S4 under three heat-treatment conditions: (a) TA 1250 °C / TB 350 °C, (b) TA 1150 °C / TB 350 °C, and (c) TA 1150 °C / TB 320 °C. The SEM images display TiMoC precipitates—elongated and aligned within the nano-bainitic matrix—and subfigure (b) marks an AlN particle. Subfigure (d) presents Fe Kα1, C Kα1,2, Ti Kα1, and Mo Lα1 maps of the region in (c), visually confirming that the bright precipitates are enriched in Ti, Mo, and C while exhibiting reduced Fe signal. The micrographs reveal denser bainite sheaf impingement at 1150 °C compared with 1250 °C, and the caption identifies the precipitates as TiMoC. The associated text reports that this longitudinal alignment reflects primary precipitation during post-casting cooling and subsequent hot deformation, with secondary precipitation playing a negligible role. Together, the images and maps confirm the Fe-depleted nature of the TiMoC complex carbides and support the conclusion that these precipitates promote bainite nucleation yet increase phase-boundary density, suppressing (Fe,M)xCy and MoC formation in steel S4.

Figure 11 presents a multi-scale microstructure characterization of low-density nano-bainitic S5 steel (Fe–0.60C–3.92Al–0.78Ni) austenitized at 1150 °C and austempered at 335 °C and 365 °C. Subfigures (a) and (b) are SEM micrographs showing nano-bainitic lamellae within lenticular martensite at 335 °C and an impingement-free structure with film-like retained austenite (γr) at 365 °C, with an inset revealing AlN and polygonal ferrite (αp) on grain boundaries. Subfigure (c) is an XRD pattern confirming only α+γr with no resolvable martensite peaks at 335 °C, comparing the two temperatures. Subfigures (d–f) are EBSD IPF maps: narrow bainitic packets with scattered martensite (M) at 335 °C and slightly coarser packets with trace allotriomorphic ferrite (F) at 365 °C. Subfigures (g–i) are corresponding KAM maps showing elevated local misorientation hotspots adjacent to martensite at 335 °C (average KAM ~0.825°) versus a marginally higher global average at 365 °C (~0.905°). Collectively, the data demonstrate that S5 transforms rapidly just above Ms (331 °C), yielding fine bainite with film-like γr; sporadic martensite at 335 °C is attributed to stress-assisted transformation and remains below XRD detectability, while the higher austempering temperature reduces strength but enhances ductility via increased γr fraction and relaxed micro-strain.
